content:
4 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

So I recently shipped a little project I’ve been calling QuizerX — a quiz app where the questions you see depend on the date you visit. Not a leaderboard, not a game with lives. Just a clean, focused quiz that rotates content based on when you show up.
Here’s the live link: quizerx.vercel.app And the code: github.com/deepakj6392/quizer
Let me walk you through the idea, the decisions I made, and what I learned along the way.

The Idea 💡
I wanted to build something simple but with a twist. Most quiz apps let you pick a topic and fire away. That’s fine, but I wanted something that felt more like a daily challenge — the kind of thing where you come back tomorrow and get something new.
The core mechanic: questions are rendered based on the date. You visit today, you get today’s set. Come back tomorrow, there’s a fresh batch. Same concept as a word-of-the-day or Wordle — the date is the seed.
No accounts. No login. No database queries per user. Just a date → question mapping.

Tech Stack 🛠️

• Next.js (TypeScript) — great for routing, and I wanted to learn more about it

• CSS — kept it custom, no heavy UI library

• Vercel — deployment was literally one push
The repo is about 84% TypeScript, which I’m honestly proud of. Typing things properly saved me from a bunch of dumb runtime bugs.

How the Date-Based Question Logic Works 📅
This is the part I got most excited about.
The idea is straightforward: you have a pool of questions, and the current date acts as a pointer into that pool. Here’s the general approach:
const today = new Date();
const dayOfYear = getDayOfYear(today); // e.g., June 28 → 179
const questionSet = questions[dayOfYear % questions.length];So if you have, say, 60 question sets, and today is day 179 of the year, users see set number 179 % 60 = 59. The next day they'll see set 0, and so on — cycling through the pool.
What I like about this:

• No backend needed — the date lives on the client, and the questions are just a static data file

• Predictable — every user on the same date sees the same quiz

• Easy to expand — just add more question sets to the pool

Building with Next.js 🚀
I chose Next.js over plain React for a few reasons:

• File-based routing — clean and zero config

• Easy static generation if I ever want to pre-render pages

• It’s what’s being used everywhere right now, so good practice
The app structure was pretty simple — a home page, a quiz page, and a results screen. State management is just React useState since there's no complex global state needed.
TypeScript made the question data structure super clean:
type Question = {
id: number;
question: string;
options: string[];
answer: string;
};Having that type enforced everywhere meant no accidental undefined answers making it to the UI.

Deployment on Vercel ⚡
This was the easiest part. Seriously — connect your GitHub repo, Vercel auto-detects Next.js, and you’re live. Every push to main triggers a new deploy.
The live URL is served at quizerx.vercel.app. Zero server management, zero DevOps headache.

What I Learned 🧠
1. Simple constraints lead to creative solutions. The “date as a seed” constraint pushed me to think differently about how to serve content without a database. It made the app faster and simpler than I expected.
2. TypeScript is worth it even for small projects. The initial overhead is real, but catching type errors during development (not at runtime in front of users) is genuinely satisfying.
3. Next.js is not overkill for small apps. I was worried it’d feel heavy for a project this size, but it was actually just… fine. The developer experience is smooth and the conventions make sense quickly.

What’s Next 🔭
A few things I’m thinking about adding:

• Topic categories — filter quizzes by JavaScript, HTML, CSS separately

• Streak tracking — using localStorage to show how many days in a row you’ve played

• Share your score — a simple card to post on Twitter/X

• Admin panel — a way to upload new question sets without touching the codebase

Wrapping Up
QuizerX started as a weekend experiment and turned into something I actually use to warm up my brain in the morning. If you’re learning web dev, try it out and see how you do!
If you want to poke around the code or contribute new question sets, the repo is open: 👉 github.com/deepakj6392/quizer
Drop a ⭐ if you find it useful — it genuinely means a lot when you’re building in public.

Built with Next.js + TypeScript · Deployed on Vercel · Open Source
